sentences.text: [INAUDIBLE] One time I paid [INAUDIBLE].
That rarely happens.
OK.
My name--
My name is David Steinweis.
I spell my last name S-T-E-I-N-W-E-I-S.
Oh, when I was a little child I went to cheder, to the Jewish school.
Then I went to public school.
And this was a town Modrzejow.
And I was safe from-- from three years old up till [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to the [INAUDIBLE].
I was living in Modrzejow.
Usually when I got older, then after the public school, I went to work.
And I used to work by my brother.
He-- he used to have a factory from clothing, like a custom tailor.
He was one from the best one, I would say, there in the-- in the whole community, Sosnoweic there all around it, little towns.
And he was my-- he was my teacher.
And I was learning.
And thank to him, I have a trade, because with this trade helped me a lot-- not always, sometimes in concentration camp.
Yeah.
Well, religious.
In-- in Poland, it was no Conservative or Reform.
It used to be maybe in the big cities was the form shuls.
Conservative, I don't know.
It was most Orthodox.
And shul, I have to go to shul.
And I have to daven every day.
And later on, I start to work, then it was a little bit different.
You know, when you're growing older, then it's different.
The child [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] understands, he got friends.
In my age, what we're going away already a little bit from home, not under the mother's care or father's care.
And you know, all youngsters are doing that.
The same life is here, it was in Poland the same thing.
And later on, I was learning the trade by my brother.
And when I knew something before, I was learning how to make pants or vests, some little things.
And then I started to work.
And I made right away money.
When I was 16 years old, I was already working Katowice.
Katowice you hear about.
It's a big city.
And from there, I worked a few years.
And then, again, by my brother, you know, I started to work good things, jackets.
And I was finish a full tailor.
And by this, what I made a living before the war.
Most of us, the Jewish kids, we didn't got Polish friends.
I talked to them, only they were not my friends.
Most of the friends were Jewish kids.
And on [?occasion?] when you pass by, you saw them.
They know me.
I said hello, how are you, this, only they were not my friends.
Many times when I was a kid, I was fighting with them, because like they were learning by the parents at home, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- they didn't behave good to the Jewish kids.
Sometimes I remember they attacked me on a bridge.
And they beat me up.
And I didn't got a chance.
Then-- and then I went to-- my brother used to have-- I crossed the bridge.
My brother got in Myslowice, he got that little factory there.
[?I don't know?] By the next day, when I went, I took something in my pocket.
And I figured when they'll attack me, then I know what to do.
There were four of them, my age, I will say.
And this time I was maybe 15 years old.
And I saw them far away.
And I was not afraid.
When I came near, they started to hit me.
I took out-- I got some bottle in my-- from-- from metal in my pocket.
And I took it out that.
And I-- I hit a boy in the nose.
I broke his nose right away.
He fall away.
He fall down.
And he was laying there.
And I ran away.
Only I was so fast, faster than them, that I was running where my brother got the factory there.
And I went in.
And that-- that's-- and this was the finish with them.
Now, we used to have-- we used to play soccer games.
Once was a soccer game and I went and I was standing.
And the same kids came.
And they came near me.
And I was not afraid, because all Jewish kids were there, grown-up people.
Then this time I was not afraid they will beat me up.
Only they didn't say a word.
And later on when they saw me, they said hello to me.
And that's all.
They never bothered me again.
The school, when I went to school in this time was the Jewish kids separate and the not-Jewish in the other place.
It was the same building, only we were-- they were teaching us in separate rooms.
And I used to have Jewish teachers, like the principal from the school was-- he was Jewish.
His name was Greenberg.
And he used to have a daughter.
Her name was Marisza.
And he got a son-- she was also a teacher.
And he got a son.
His name was Tadek.
And he was also a teacher, only the son didn't work in the school.
He was-- he was working some other place.
Only the Marisza, she was my teacher.
And I used to have a other teacher.
Her name was Saksowna.
And she used to-- to live in other village, not far where I used to go to school.
It was a walk maybe 20 minutes that she came every day to school there.
And when I finished school, I have to go to work.
See, it was not like here.
[INAUDIBLE].
It was then big cities, rich people, they were in business or they have good jobs, they send the children to high school or-- or to-- to college.
Only it was maybe one from 10,000 what you went to college.
Or some people got relatives in England or they got in Germany or they got here in the United States.
They sent the-- the kids away to-- for education.
See, I am from a family from seven children.
My mother used to have nine children, only seven they were alive.
And my father used to work in a mill, flour mill.
OK.
He made a living.
He was working very hard.
I remember he brought home flour from the-- from his place.
And my mother used to bake for the children bread or some challah or some other things, you know.
My mother was a housewife.
And she got plenty work to do at home with seven children.
At home, I was the youngest.
And then when they got older, the-- the oldest, they got married, then the second one, the third one, you know, and the fourth one.
And I was the only one what-- I was very young.
Then when I was 21 years, I went to the army.
And when I started out, me, I went away, this was in 1938, March, 15.
And I was-- I was in the army 18 months, a year and a half.
And in Poland, who was 21 years old a man, he was healthy, Jewish or not Jewish, he got to go to the army.
Then I was healthy.
And they took me in.
I was in the army 18 months.
And it came time to go home, the war broke out.
Then I was already on the front there in-- in Silesia, Upper Silesia, in a place they called this, the town [INAUDIBLE].
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Yes.
Well, you see, where I was living was-- it was not a big town.
It was Sosnowiec, really the big city.
And this was maybe three kilometer away.
And the people there, what I was raised with them, not Jewish, they didn't bother the youngsters, or the friends.
And only we were sometimes attacked from Myslowice.
Over there was already more anti-Semites.
And from the Germans came out later on in 1933, '34 with the Nazis-- start with the Nazis.
And there was the Hitlerjugend.
And many times we want to go where you want to have some fun.
In our town, we could have played soccer on the fields.
On some other fun, to go to a park or to a movies, we have to cross the bridge and to go to Myslowice.
Many times they attacked us.
We didn't-- I didn't go by myself.
It happened not every day.
No.
It's happened most on the weekends.
Then he went in groups, three, four.
And they used to attack us.
And we were fighting physically with them.
Fists--
Fists.
Fist.
No.
Fist.
Fist.
Yeah. 'Til when they saw that we are stronger, they escaped.
When they saw we are even, they stayed, and then they retreat.
It was not so easy with them.

And we-- we were feeling like second-class citizen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because the Jew could not work even with the post office or some government jobs or let's say anything but policemen or anything what got to do with a job from the city was very hard to get it.
Even for us was coal mines.
And it was some Jewish people what they wanted to work on the-- in the coal mines.
They used to pay I would not say very good wages, only people used to make their living.
Then I knew-- I knew somebody, two of them.
And they went in the office there on the coal mine.
They used to have a house there.
And they were asking for a job.
And they told them open, we are very sorry, we can't hire Jewish people.
And then we got there other factory, not far in the other town.
Same thing, they didn't hire Jewish people.
What the Jew got, the youngsters, to learn, is a trade.
Could be a carpenter, a barber, a tailor, a shoemaker, or a barber.
What else?
Anything trade, we would learn.
Yeah.
We have to be very careful.
See, nighttime, we didn't walk in places where we knew it's dangerous there.
We didn't go there.
Only nighttime, let's say, 'til 11 o'clock or 12 o'clock, we knew where to walk, only among Jews, where Jewish people used to live.
See, it's-- this was already in the blood.
And-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were hating the Jewish people all over, see, no matter what city in Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK.
Some people, when they needed the Jew, let's say they do business with him, like they want to sell something or to buy something, they were very good, very fine.
It-- it was-- they used to say, we used to joke.
I will say a few words in Polish.
[SPEAKING POLISH] This means when it's no good, you have to go to the Jew.
The Jew will help out, you know, something like that.

And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they could not be my friends, only not my friends, not to the Jewish people friends, because it was-- I remember it was some Polack and he talked the Jewish language, like me, because he was raised with the Jewish people.
And to him, we talked even Jewish.
And many time if we talked to them-- talked to this person-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] his name was Yusek Karbowniczek.
And everybody knew him.
He was a guy he went around among the Jewish people.
And he talked, everything.
Many time even I ask him.
He was-- but you, we can do everything, we talk.
Why do other people hate us?
He didn't give an answer.
He said, ah, this goes back 400 years, maybe-- maybe 300 years, maybe 1,000 years, he said.
And I ask him, comes-- this comes from the church?
Sometimes, he said, yes.
Sometimes not.
It depends who's preaching the church, he said.
I remember used to have there a church.
And Sunday, you know, like here, people went to the church.
And they used to have two police there, two.
One was a friend of the Jewish people.
And he used to have a motorcycle on three wheels.
And let's say my father used to walk to Sosnowiec, three kilometer.
And when he passed by with the motorcycle, he picked him up.
And he was very good.
Only later on came a other one.
And they got-- not a physical fight, only who got to remain on the job.
And who do you think remained on the job?
The other fellow, not the man who was with the Jewish people together, the priest.
This I remember we were talking always about that.
At the dinner table, did your father used to talk to you folks about how the world was changing and how it was getting closer to war?
Did you guys talk about it among the family?
About the war?
Yeah.
When the war-- OK.
This time when I was in the army--
It was maybe closer 22, because you are 21, you were before the doctor.
Still, they take you is already 22, see, Sometimes 21 1/2.
It depends.
Yeah.
It was a must.
This was the law to go.
If I will refuse to go to the army, I could get five years in prison.
Yeah.
In the army, we were feeling already in 1938, not from the beginning, around October, November, December, that the war will come, because I was talking to the officers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I used to have a job in the-- in the army.
I was working in a magazine.
I gave out clothing to the soldiers.
Because I was a tailor, they needed me to work there.
Then I worked there.
And they used to come in there for clothing.
And I used to fix for them.
And I gave them.
They got to tell me a few days before, and I prepared for them.
And they told me, David, war-- war is coming.
And I told them that I see the different-- in the exercise what the soldiers are doing.
We went out every day there on exercise there on the fields.
And when they came in, the recruits, they-- they were teaching them like a soldier who was already a half year.
And many times I asked him, why you teaching them shooting here, shooting there.
And those things I was learning when-- after three months.
They told me war-- war's coming.
And we hear already in the newspapers, the radios.
We didn't got television this time.
And they were preparing for war.
Only nobody we will expect that something-- a war like this, because always were wars soldier against soldier, not a soldier again civilians.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Only it-- it was very, very bad.
And then two weeks before the broke out, I came home.
It was not allowed to go home this time already, four weeks before the war broke out.
Only I asked my officer.
He said, OK.
You can go home for three, four days, only don't forget to come back.
And I went home.
And everybody asked me, what's going on there in the army?
What's going on?
I said, it's no good.
I feel that's going to be war.
And then the war broke out.
When the war broke out, I was already on the field two days before.
We went out.
I was in the Upper Silesia division.
A division got, I will say, 10,000 soldiers.
And used to be in groups, you know.
We called this a polk.
And three --three polks together, this was a division.
And then we went.
I remember it was on a Thursday.
Friday morning, it broke out the war.
And Thursday morning, we already went to that field, the name was [INAUDIBLE], in the woods.
We were there, 4,000 soldiers, only let's say three kilometer, 4,000 soldier.
In the other places after this was soldiers too.
The whole border line from Germany was sealed from Polish soldiers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Only when the war started Friday morning, that civilian attached us.
They used to have bands on their hands.
And they called them Volksdeutsche.
Volksdeutsche means they are the Germans, you know.
And they attacked us from the trees.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And we were standing before the woods with officers.
The sergeant, he was my sergeant a year and a half and I knew him good.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why I knew him so good better than everyone else?
I worked for him.
He took me home.
He uses to have-- his wife was sick.
And I was playing with the kids there.
He got there three children.
And he talked to his wife German.
And I understood German.
I said, I'm surprised that you talked German.
And this, I suspect him that he got to do something with the Germans.
In the war, I'm coming back, when they start to shoot on us, the Germans, he took off his hat.
And he threw it in the air.
And he said, let's live long Poland.
Like in Polish, it's [SPEAKING POLISH].
And the minute he threw the this hat maybe two floor high, it came down.
We were surrounded from all over.
And they killed a lot of soldiers this time.
He gave them the signal.
And they were on trees and they saw there us.
Then the officers took over.
And they send us in-- in the woods.
But I run again in the woods.
It was a lot of dead soldiers.
And I was falling right away.
And I was rolling, I remember.
And when we left-- we were fighting there-- we started the war Friday morning.

The whole day, the whole night Saturday, without food, without everything, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Saturday the whole day, Saturday night, the whole night till 3 o'clock in the morning when it start to rain.
When it starts to rain, we were in the-- in the-- you know?
How you call this?
In the-- Trenches?
Yeah.
Down there, we got holes they made to-- to hide and to shoot from there.
Yeah.
